overview.whyItMatters: Foundation models are only as good as the data they receive. The principle of 'garbage in, garbage out' applies strongly to GenAI—malformed inputs, missing data, and quality issues all degrade FM outputs. Data validation ensures inputs meet quality standards; processing pipelines transform raw data into FM-ready formats. The exam tests your ability to build robust data workflows that catch problems early, transform diverse data types, and prepare inputs that maximize FM effectiveness.

concepts.title: Data Quality for GenAI
concepts.content:
Foundation model inputs require specific quality standards, and violations often fail silently—the FM produces bad output rather than throwing an error. Proactive validation catches problems before they waste inference costs or deliver poor results to users.

Format validation ensures inputs have correct structure. Text must be properly encoded (UTF-8), free of binary artifacts, and within expected length limits. JSON payloads must parse correctly. Images must be in supported formats with valid dimensions. These seem basic, but real-world data pipelines encounter every edge case imaginable.

Completeness checking detects missing required fields. If your prompt template expects a customer name and purchase history, validate that both are present before constructing the prompt. Missing data leads to generic, unhelpful responses or, worse, hallucinations where the FM invents missing information.

Anomaly detection identifies unexpected values. A product price of -$5.00 or a date in year 2099 signals data corruption that should stop processing. Domain-specific rules catch issues that generic validation misses.

AWS Glue Data Quality provides declarative rule definitions using DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language). Define rules like 'price > 0' or 'email matches pattern' and apply them to batch data. Rules run automatically during Glue ETL jobs, failing pipelines when quality thresholds aren't met.

SageMaker Data Wrangler offers visual data preparation with 300+ built-in transformations. It's excellent for exploratory work—understanding data distributions, identifying quality issues, and building transformation recipes that can be exported to production pipelines.

For real-time validation, Lambda functions implement custom logic. Check inputs against your specific business rules before forwarding to FMs. This catches issues that generic tools miss.
keyPoints: Format validation: Correct encoding, expected structure, valid JSON
Completeness: All required fields present, no truncation
Anomaly detection: Outliers, impossible values, corrupted data
Constraint enforcement: Length limits, allowed values, data type checks
concepts.examTip: Questions mentioning 'data quality rules' or 'validation before processing' typically point to Glue Data Quality. For real-time validation, Lambda is the answer.

concepts.title: Multimodal Data Processing
concepts.content:
Modern foundation models accept multiple input types—text, images, documents, audio—but each modality requires specific preprocessing. Understanding these requirements is essential for building applications that leverage multimodal capabilities.

Image processing prepares visual inputs for multimodal FMs. Images must be resized to reasonable dimensions (models have resolution limits), converted to supported formats (JPEG, PNG), and encoded as base64 strings for API transmission. Compression reduces payload size without significantly impacting model understanding. For Bedrock multimodal models, images are included directly in the message content.

Document processing extracts text and structure from complex documents. PDFs, Word documents, and scanned images require specialized handling. Amazon Textract performs OCR (Optical Character Recognition) on images and PDFs, extracting text while preserving layout information. For structured documents like forms and tables, Textract can identify fields and their values. This extracted text then feeds into text-based FM processing.

Audio processing converts speech to text for language model consumption. Amazon Transcribe handles speech-to-text conversion with support for multiple languages, speaker identification, and custom vocabularies. Real-time transcription enables live applications; batch transcription handles recorded audio efficiently.

Video processing combines multiple modalities. Extract key frames as images for visual analysis. Transcribe the audio track for spoken content. Some advanced workflows analyze both visual and audio content, then synthesize insights.

Build modality-specific preprocessing stages in your pipeline. SageMaker Processing jobs handle batch transformations at scale. Lambda functions process individual items in real-time. Step Functions orchestrates multi-stage pipelines that combine different preprocessing steps.

Error handling is critical for multimodal pipelines. Images may be corrupted, audio may be silent or garbled, documents may be password-protected. Design your pipeline to handle these gracefully—skip bad inputs, log issues, and continue processing rather than failing the entire batch.
keyPoints: Images: Resize, compress, base64 encode, validate dimensions
Documents: Extract text via Textract, preserve structure when possible
Audio: Transcribe to text using Amazon Transcribe
Video: Extract frames + transcribe audio track
concepts.examTip: When scenarios involve documents or images, look for Textract (documents) or Bedrock multimodal models (images). Transcribe handles audio.

concepts.title: API-Ready Data Formatting
concepts.content:
Foundation model APIs expect specific input formats. Understanding these formats and building reliable serialization is essential for integrating FMs into applications.

Bedrock's Messages API uses a conversation format with roles and content. Messages have 'user' or 'assistant' roles, and content can include text and images. System messages establish context and constraints. This format is consistent across models but each model may have specific limits and supported features.

The structure looks like: {messages: [{role: 'user', content: [{type: 'text', text: '...'}]}]}. Getting this structure wrong—missing brackets, wrong field names, improper nesting—causes API errors that may be cryptic. Build robust serialization that handles edge cases.

Token limits require input management. If your input exceeds the model's context limit, the request fails. Either truncate input to fit (losing information) or chunk it and process in multiple calls (adding complexity). Know your model's limits and design accordingly.

Special characters need escaping in JSON. Newlines, quotes, backslashes, and control characters must be properly escaped or they'll break JSON parsing. Use proper JSON serialization libraries rather than string concatenation.

SageMaker endpoints have custom payload schemas. When using SageMaker-hosted models, the input format depends on how the endpoint was configured. Document your expected schemas and validate inputs against them.

Build a consistent formatting layer in your application. Rather than constructing JSON throughout your codebase, create utility functions that handle serialization reliably. This prevents formatting bugs and makes API changes easier to manage.
keyPoints: Bedrock Messages API: Roles (user/assistant) + content array format
Token limits: Know model limits, truncate or chunk as needed
Escaping: Handle special characters properly in JSON
Consistency: Build a formatting layer rather than ad-hoc serialization

concepts.title: Input Enhancement Techniques
concepts.content:
Raw user input often benefits from preprocessing that improves FM response quality. Rather than sending exactly what the user typed, enhance and enrich inputs to give the FM better context for generating useful responses.

Entity extraction identifies important elements in user input. Amazon Comprehend extracts entities (names, dates, locations, organizations), key phrases, and sentiment. This extracted information can route requests to appropriate handlers, filter responses, or be injected into prompts for context.

Text normalization fixes common input issues. Expand abbreviations ('u' → 'you'), correct obvious misspellings, standardize date formats, and remove excessive punctuation. The FM handles imperfect input, but clean input often produces cleaner output.

Context enrichment adds relevant background information. If a user asks about 'my order,' inject their recent order details into the prompt. If they reference a product by nickname, resolve it to the full product name. This context enables more specific, helpful responses.

Query expansion adds related terms to improve retrieval. For RAG applications, a query about 'compute options' might be expanded to include 'EC2,' 'Lambda,' 'Fargate'—terms likely to appear in relevant documents. This bridges vocabulary gaps between how users ask questions and how documents are written.

Personalization adapts prompts based on user characteristics. Include relevant user history, preferences, or role information in the prompt. A technical user might get detailed explanations; a casual user might get simpler responses.

These enhancements run before FM invocation, typically in Lambda functions. They're preprocessing steps that transform raw input into optimized prompts. Track which enhancements improve output quality—not all preprocessing is beneficial, and some may add latency without meaningful improvement.
keyPoints: Entity extraction: Identify names, dates, locations using Comprehend
Normalization: Fix spelling, expand abbreviations, standardize formats
Context injection: Add user history, relevant documents, preferences
Query expansion: Add synonyms and related terms for retrieval

skillExplanations.explanation:
Data quality workflows validate inputs before expensive FM processing. Catching bad data early prevents wasted inference costs and poor user experiences.

Glue Data Quality defines rules declaratively using DQDL. Rules can check completeness (required fields present), uniqueness (no duplicates), validity (values match patterns), and custom conditions. Rules integrate into Glue ETL jobs, failing pipelines when quality falls below thresholds.

SageMaker Data Wrangler provides visual data preparation. Its interface enables quick exploration—view distributions, identify anomalies, understand data characteristics. Data Wrangler recipes can be exported to production pipelines, bridging exploratory analysis and automated processing.

Lambda implements custom validation for real-time inputs. Check business-specific rules that generic tools can't express: verify referenced products exist in your catalog, ensure prices fall within valid ranges, confirm customer IDs match authenticated users. This application-specific validation catches issues domain-generic tools miss.

Design validation to fail fast. If input is invalid, reject it immediately rather than letting it propagate through expensive processing only to produce garbage output. Return clear error messages that help users fix their inputs.

awsServices.whenToUse: Declarative quality rules for batch data processing. Automated monitoring and alerting. Integration with Glue ETL pipelines.
Visual data exploration and preparation. Quick understanding of data characteristics. Recipe development for production.
Real-time custom validation. Business-specific rules. Per-request input checking.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Multimodal processing converts diverse input types into formats foundation models can consume. Each modality has specific requirements and processing techniques.

Bedrock multimodal models accept images directly. Claude and other vision-capable models process images as part of message content. Images must be base64-encoded and within size limits. The model analyzes visual content and can answer questions about images, extract text, describe scenes, and more.

SageMaker Processing jobs handle batch transformations at scale. When you need to preprocess thousands of images or documents, Processing jobs run on managed infrastructure with automatic scaling. This is more cost-effective than Lambda for high-volume batch work.

Amazon Textract extracts text from documents with structure preservation. For forms and tables, Textract identifies fields and values, maintaining relationships that simple OCR loses. This extracted, structured data feeds into FM prompts for document analysis, comparison, or summarization.

Amazon Transcribe converts audio to text with speaker identification and timestamp alignment. Custom vocabularies improve accuracy for domain-specific terminology. Real-time streaming supports live applications; batch mode handles recorded content.

awsServices.whenToUse: Multimodal FMs that natively accept images with text. Vision capabilities for image analysis.
Batch transformation jobs at scale. Heavy preprocessing of large datasets.
Extract text from documents and images. Form and table structure preservation.
Convert audio to text. Speech recognition with speaker identification.

skillExplanations.explanation:
API-ready formatting transforms your application data into structures the FM APIs expect. Correct formatting is essential—malformed requests fail, and subtle formatting issues can degrade output quality.

Bedrock's Messages API provides a consistent interface across models. Understand the message structure: roles (system, user, assistant), content types (text, image), and model-specific parameters (temperature, max_tokens). Build utilities that construct valid messages reliably.

SageMaker endpoints accept custom payload formats defined during deployment. When you deploy models to SageMaker, you control the input schema. Document expected formats and validate inputs against schemas before sending requests.

Token counting is essential for managing context limits. Before sending a request, estimate token count and handle cases where input exceeds limits. Options include truncation (simple but loses data), summarization (preserves meaning but adds processing), or chunking with aggregation (complex but complete).

Error handling must gracefully manage formatting failures. Parse errors, encoding issues, and unexpected responses all occur in production. Build robust error handling that logs issues, returns meaningful errors to users, and enables debugging.

awsServices.whenToUse: Standard FM invocation with Messages API format. Consistent interface across multiple models.
Custom endpoints with application-specific payload formats.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Input enhancement improves FM responses by enriching raw user input with context, structure, and additional information. These preprocessing steps optimize prompts without changing the underlying model.

Amazon Comprehend provides entity extraction and NLP analysis. Extract entities (people, places, organizations, dates), detect sentiment, identify key phrases, and classify topics. This information can route requests, filter responses, or provide context in prompts.

Lambda functions implement custom enhancement logic. Normalize text, expand abbreviations, inject user context, enrich with external data, or transform inputs into consistent formats. Custom code enables business-specific enhancements that generic tools can't provide.

Context injection adds relevant background to prompts. Retrieve user history, related documents, or domain knowledge, and include it in the prompt. This gives the FM information it needs to generate relevant, personalized responses.

Measure enhancement effectiveness. Not all preprocessing improves outcomes—some may add latency without benefit. A/B test enhanced vs. raw inputs to quantify improvement. Focus enhancement effort where it demonstrably helps.

awsServices.whenToUse: Extract entities, sentiment, and key phrases from text. NLP analysis of user input.
Custom text processing and normalization. Context injection and enrichment.

values.Glue Data Quality: Batch data validation with declarative rules
values.SageMaker Data Wrangler: Visual data exploration and recipe development

values.Glue Data Quality: Batch processing integrated with Glue ETL
Systematic quality monitoring for data lakes

examStrategies.approach: Glue Data Quality for batch validation with declarative rules on data lakes and ETL pipelines. Lambda for real-time custom validation per request. Look for keywords indicating batch vs. real-time to choose between them.
Textract extracts text from documents and images. Bedrock multimodal models analyze images directly. Transcribe converts audio to text. Match the input modality to the appropriate service.
Amazon Comprehend handles entity extraction, sentiment analysis, and key phrase extraction. It's more cost-effective than using FMs for these standard NLP tasks.

keyTakeaways: Validate data BEFORE sending to FMs—catching errors early saves cost and improves quality
Glue Data Quality provides declarative rules for batch data validation
Comprehend extracts entities and sentiment efficiently—cheaper than FM inference
Multimodal processing: Textract for documents, Transcribe for audio, Bedrock for images
Build consistent JSON formatting utilities rather than ad-hoc serialization
commonMistakes: Skipping validation and relying on FM error handling (FMs produce bad output, not errors)
Using expensive FMs for tasks like entity extraction that Comprehend handles better
Forgetting to handle token limits—inputs exceeding limits fail or truncate
Not encoding images properly for multimodal models (base64, correct format)
Building formatting logic throughout codebase instead of centralized utilities
